title: Recurring Revenue Design

content.question: What part of your business could become recurring revenue within 90 days?
content.life_lens: Systems that earn while you sleep.
sop: List every product or service you currently sell as a one-time transaction.
Identify which ones solve a problem that recurs, not a problem solved once and done.
Package the recurring-need offer into a subscription or membership tier with a clear cadence.
Rate your retention realistically, 0-100% (see calculation, page 5), based on how many subscribers stay past month 3 in similar offers.
Optimize the weakest lever first: more customers, higher price, or better retention, whichever moves recurring revenue most.
questions: What part of your business could become recurring revenue within 90 days?
If you got zero new customers next month, would revenue drop to zero, or would something keep paying you?
Which one-time offer gets repurchased anyway, and why isn't it already a subscription?

definitions.def: The percentage of customers who keep paying or using a product over time.

content.equation_example: Customers (count) times Price (dollars/month) times Retention (%) equals MRR. 200 customers times $50 times 90% retention equals $9,000 MRR. Higher retention or price raises MRR fastest without needing new customers; lower retention quietly erodes revenue you already earned.
content.closing_statement: Sell it once, get paid on repeat.

caption:
Principle Nº 170: Recurring Revenue Design

One sale, infinite income.

Ask yourself: What part of your business could become recurring revenue within 90 days?
